content_en:
Al-Isabah fi Tamyiz as-Sahabah is organized into eight sections (aqsam) that reflect Ibn Hajar's precise methodological classification of individuals associated with the Prophet Muhammad. The first and most important section covers those who are established as Companions by reliable evidence. The second covers those who are mentioned in the texts only as Companions without further documentation. The third covers those about whom there is doubt whether they are Companions or Successors. The fourth covers those who have been misidentified as Companions. Additional sections cover women Companions, children who met the Prophet, and those who converted after the Prophet's death but during the period of the Companions.

This careful classificatory structure reflects Ibn Hajar's hadith-critical methodology. The definition of a Sahabi (Companion) is not merely anyone who met the Prophet — it specifically means someone who met the Prophet while believing in him and died as a Muslim. Ibn Hajar's classification scheme allows him to include everyone associated with the Prophet while clearly marking the degree of certainty about each person's status.

For each entry, Ibn Hajar follows a standard format: the person's name and genealogy, how they are connected to the Prophet, the relevant hadith and historical reports documenting this connection, scholarly assessments of those reports' reliability, and any biographical information available about the person's life and activities. Entries range from a few lines for obscure individuals to many pages for major Companions.

Ibn Hajar's critical apparatus — his evaluation of the reliability of sources, his identification of errors in earlier works, his reconciliation of conflicting accounts — is applied throughout. He regularly corrects mistakes in earlier treatments of the Companions, notes when accounts are confused or when two individuals have been merged into one, and evaluates the evidence with the technical precision of the hadith sciences.

The alphabetical arrangement within each section facilitates reference use. Ibn Hajar includes separate alphabetical sections for individuals known by their given names, those known by their fathers' names, those known by their mothers' names, and those known by a nickname (kunya or laqab). This multi-index approach ensures that a person can be found regardless of how they are typically referenced.

The work draws on a vast bibliography. Ibn Hajar cites hundreds of earlier sources — the major hadith collections, specialized biographical works on the Companions, local histories, and general biographical dictionaries — synthesizing information scattered across an enormous literature into a single comprehensive reference.
